figures.caption: Figure 4: Dimensionless representation of the inter-granular constitutive relationships including unloading/reloading branches: (a) normalized normal force-displacement relationship and (b) normalized shear force-displacement relationship. The axes are non-dimensionalized using the initial normal stiffness A and average grain size l. The characteristic peak forces and their corresponding displacements are explicitly identified by the vector of dimensionless parameters ξ = (P1, P2, P3, P4).

Figure 8: Visualization of surrogate model predictions on the test data. Predictions of the model trained with the curvature penalty are shown for (a) ten randomly selected cases and (b) ten cases with the largest relative MSE. Predictions of the model trained without the curvature penalty are shown for (c) the same ten cases as in (b) and (d) ten cases with the largest relative MSE.

Figure 9: Representative test cases illustrating the predicted failure envelopes. Blue curves denote the surrogate model trained without curvature regularization, red curves denote the model with the proposed curvature regularization, and black open circles denote the reference data.
Figure 3: Schematic illustration of the architecture used to parametrize the surrogate models.

Figure 7: Training curves and error distributions of the surrogate models. (a) Training and test loss histories with the curvature penalty term, (b) histograms of relative MSE on train and test data with the curvature penalty term, (c) training and test loss histories without the curvature penalty term, (d) and histograms of relative MSE on train and test data without the curvature penalty term.

Figure 12: Inverse identification of micromechanical parameters on a representative case with non-convexity. (a) Ten trajectories of micromechanical parameters P1 & P2 during the gradient descent. Initial guesses are marked as open squares, converged parameters as triangles, and the target failure envelope as a star. (b) Loss histories of the ten trials. (c) Ten corresponding surrogate outputs as solid lines, and the data as black open circles. (d) Ten corresponding micromechanical solver outputs as solid lines, and the data as black open circles.
Figure 6: Histories of the relative MSE and the fraction of negative curvature values, comparing the FD and AD schemes for different batch sizes. (a) Batch size 100 and (b) 1,000.

Figure 13: Comparison of data efficiency between LHS and adaptive sequential sampling. Here, AL denotes datasets obtained using the adaptive sequential sampling algorithm. (a) shows the relative MSE of surrogate models trained on adaptively acquired data and on LHS data, reported as the mean across realizations. (b) reports the corresponding relative MSE in terms of the 99% quantile across realizations. These statistics are averaged over three trials, with error bars indicating the minimum and maximum values across trials. (c) shows the loss histories of models trained on acquired datasets of sizes 10, 80, and 640, with three trials shown for each dataset size.

Figure 11: Inverse identification of micromechanical parameters. (a) Ten trajectories of micromechanical parameters P1 & P2 during the gradient descent. Initial guesses are marked as open squares, converged parameters as triangles, and the target failure envelope as a star. (b) Loss histories of the ten trials. (c) Ten corresponding surrogate outputs as solid lines, and the data as black open circles. (d) Ten corresponding micromechanical solver outputs as solid lines, and the data as black open circles.

Figure 5: Comparison of the computational time of the FD and AD schemes across different batch sizes. Results for (a) 100 and (b) 1,000 evaluation points are shown. Markers indicate the median over 100 trials, where error bars denote the 25% and 75% quantiles.

Figure 10: (a) Distribution of the signed curvature in the given data. Distributions of the signed curvature for the trained surrogate models, (b) without the curvature penalty and (c) with the curvature penalty. The values shown in parentheses indicate the fraction of negative signed curvature values.
